summary:
The domestication of the olive (Olea europaea) from wild oleaster populations in the eastern Mediterranean — primarily in the Levant, southern Anatolia, and possibly the Aegean — began during the Neolithic period, with the earliest evidence of olive exploitation and possibly cultivation dating to around 6000 to 5000 BCE. Wild olives grow naturally throughout the Mediterranean basin, and their oil-rich fruit would have been a valuable food and fuel source for Neolithic communities long before formal cultivation began.

The transition from exploiting wild olives to managing and eventually cultivating olive trees is difficult to document precisely because the genetic and morphological changes associated with domestication in long-lived perennial plants accumulate slowly and are harder to detect than those in annual cereals. Macrobotanical evidence of olive exploitation appears at Levantine sites during the seventh and sixth millennia BCE, and by the Chalcolithic period there is clearer evidence of intensified olive use associated with oil extraction. Olive oil became one of the most economically and culturally important commodities of the ancient Mediterranean world.

The olive's significance extends far beyond its caloric value. Olive oil served as fuel for lamps, as a lubricant, as a cosmetic and medicine, and as a preservative for other foods. In ancient Greek and Roman civilization, olives and olive oil were central to economy, diet, and religious practice — the olive tree was sacred to the goddess Athena and symbolized prosperity and peace. The roots of this cultural centrality lie in the Neolithic communities of the Levant who first began to systematically manage and cultivate this remarkable tree.
